input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to benchmark three methods (AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, UniqueWordCount) from a TextAnalyzer class, comparing .NET 9 vs .NET 10 performance. Let me start by exploring the codebase and checking what .NET versions are available.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "dotnet --list-sdks && echo \"---\" && dotnet --list-runtimes", "description": "Check available .NET SDKs and runtimes", "initial_wait": 10}

+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.reasoningText: BenchmarkDotNet handles multi-targeting itself - I need to pick one framework to compile the harness under, and BDN will launch the benchmarks on both runtimes. Let me use the latest SDK (net10.0) to build the harness.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-00014a5352d94263987b4935895a1cd7/Benchmarks && dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 -- --filter '*' 2>&1", "description": "Run benchmarks with net10.0 framework", "initial_wait": 120}

data.content: The BDN version doesn't know `Net100` yet. Let me fix the config.
data.reasoningText:
The BenchmarkDotNet version doesn't have RuntimeMoniker.Net100 yet. Let me check what's available or use a different approach. I can use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute with custom `EnvironmentInfo` or use a manual config. Let me check what version of BenchmarkDotNet was installed and what monikers are available.

Actually, for newer .NET versions not yet known to BDN, I should use a custom config with `Job.Default.WithRuntime(CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0"))`.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't support .NET 10 yet. I'll write a manual benchmark harness instead.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.x doesn't support .NET 10 yet. Let me try a different approach - I'll use the latest prerelease version of BenchmarkDotNet that might support .NET 10, or I'll write a manual benchmark harness instead. Given the time constraint, a manual benchmark is faster.

Actually, let me check if there's a newer BDN version available. Or I could try adding the `SdkValidation` to be ignored. Let me try adding `DisableOptimizationsValidator` and also the SDK validator.

Actually the problem is the SDK validator, not the optimizations validator. 

I think the best path forward is to just write a simple manual benchmark since getting BenchmarkDotNet working with .NET 10 is taking too long.

data.content:
<system_notification>
Shell command "Run benchmarks on both frameworks" (shellId: 343) has completed successfully. Use read_bash with shellId "343" to retrieve the output.
</system_notification>
The results completed but the numbers look suspiciously skewed — the machine may have been under heavier load during the .NET 9 run. Let me re-run in reverse order to validate.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-00014a5352d94263987b4935895a1cd7/Benchmarks && echo "=== Run 2: .NET 10 first ===" && echo "--- .NET 10 ---" && dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 2>&1 && echo && echo "--- .NET 9 ---" && dotnet run -c Release --framework net9.0 2>&1

data.reasoningText:
The results look dramatic - .NET 9 numbers are suspiciously slow (605μs for AverageWordLength?). This is likely because the machine is under load from other processes, as the user warned. But the relative differences are still very large. Let me think about this...

Actually, 100,000 iterations of AverageWordLength over 10,000 words... that's 10,000 iterations through ReadOnlyCollection per call. ReadOnlyCollection access through the indexer involves interface dispatch. In .NET 10, there may have been improvements to devirtualization or ReadOnlyCollection performance.

Wait, but 605μs per call for iterating over 10,000 ints seems very slow even for .NET 9. 

That works out to roughly 60 nanoseconds per element access, which suggests the machine is definitely under load. Since the user mentioned this could happen, I should probably run the benchmarks again to see if the results are consistent - the differences are so stark (40x for AverageWordLength, 21x for WordLengthHistogram, 2.7x for UniqueWordCount) that I want to verify they're not just noise from system load.

Let me flip the order and run .NET 10 first, then .NET 9, to see if the sequence itself is affecting the results.
